draft.introduction: The Lankavatara Sutra is a Mahayana Buddhist text of enormous influence, particularly in the Chan/Zen traditions. Composed in Sanskrit, probably between the 4th and 5th centuries CE, it was the text Bodhidharma reportedly transmitted to his successors as containing the essence of the Buddha’s teaching. Chapter 2 presents the Buddha’s discourse on self-realisation (pratyatma, “one’s own inner awareness”)—the direct knowledge that transcends all conceptual categories. The text’s central claim is that “noble wisdom is beyond all categories.” This is not anti-intellectual obscurantism but a precise philosophical position: the deepest reality cannot be captured by language or concepts because it is prior to the distinctions that language requires. The parallel to apophatic theology in the Christian tradition is direct: both Pseudo-Dionysius and the Lankavatara agree that the ultimate is beyond affirmation and negation alike.

translation.text: Then Mahamati the bodhisattva said to the Blessed One: “You speak of self-realisation as the domain of noble wisdom. What is this self-realisation, and how is it attained?” The Blessed One replied: “When the projections of the mind cease and the habit-energy of beginningless false imagination is exhausted, then one attains self-realisation. It is not attained through words, through study, or through logical analysis.”
translation.original_text: अथ खलु महामतिर्बोधिसत्त्वो भगवन्तमेतदवोचत्। प्रत्यात्मगतिं आर्यज्ञानगोचरं वदसि।

translation.text: Noble wisdom (aryajnana) is beyond all categories. It is not being and not non-being. It is not eternal and not impermanent. It is not cause and not effect. It is beyond the reach of mind and language. It is realised within oneself, by oneself—it cannot be communicated from one person to another, any more than one can describe the taste of honey to one who has never tasted it.
translation.original_text: आर्यज्ञानं सर्वविकल्पातीतम्। न भावो नाभावः। न शाश्वतं नाशाश्वतम्।

translation.text: All things are manifestations of mind. When this is understood, external objects are seen to be non-existent, and the mind itself, freed from the habit of grasping, returns to its original state of luminous clarity. This is not the destruction of mind but its purification. The waves cease, but the ocean remains. What remains is the Tathagata-garbha—the womb of Buddhahood present in all beings from the very beginning.

translator_notes.note: The claim that noble wisdom is “beyond all categories” places the Lankavatara squarely in the Madhyamaka tradition of Nagarjuna, while its emphasis on self-realisation aligns it with the Yogacara school. The text synthesises both approaches: reality is empty of inherent existence (Madhyamaka) and simultaneously the luminous nature of mind itself (Yogacara).

The Tathagata-garbha (“womb of Buddhahood”) doctrine is the closest Buddhist analogue to the Christian concept of the divine image in humanity. It asserts that all sentient beings possess the seed or matrix of awakening within them. Liberation is not the acquisition of something foreign but the uncovering of what was always present. The parallel to Mark the Hermit’s baptismal theology—where theosis is the manifestation of a grace already given—is striking.
